coredata.dc:title: Fathoming into the dynamic interactions on Tetraaminophenylporphyrin hybrid systems via photophysical studies assisted by computational techniques and their role in dye sensitized solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Pallikkara, Athira
Ramakrishnan, Kala
Srinivasan, Lakshmi
Jayasree, Elambalassery G.
coredata.dc:description: Amongst the umpteen solar cells, dye sensitized solar cells by their low cost and eco-friendly nature, have a seat of their own. Photosensitizers is the pioneer component from which the entire process begins. Herein, Tetraaminophenylporphyrin hybrids in a series bearing with graphene quantum dot, 1-pyrenecarboxylic acid and perylenetetracarboxylic acid labeled as TAPP:GQD, TAPP:PCA and TAPP:PTCA were used as photosensitizers in DSSCs. Incipiently, the photophysical studies of these systems have been carried out and the feasibility of electron transfer from Tetraaminophenylporphyrin to the second component in the systems is acknowledged thereby. It is reckoned that photoinduced electron transfer with dynamic quenching mechanism is the impetus for the photophysical properties associated with TAPP:PTCA system, while it is Förster Resonance Energy Transfer in TAPP:PCA. Computational calculations with the aid of Density Functional Theory, Atoms-in-Molecules topology analysis and decomposition analysis elaborates the concept of non-covalent interactions within the hybrids. Following to that, DSSCs are fabricated and the appraised efficiencies of the devices show a pattern of TAPP:PCA > TAPP:PTCA > TAPP:GQDs. This work mainly focusses on the molecular level understanding of the electron transfer dynamics within the hybrid systems and thereby developing strategies in future for composing organic/inorganic hybrids with enhanced charge transfer ability, leading to higher efficiency.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dye sensitized solar cells
Porphyrins
Electron transfer
Photosensitizers

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of the interactions of TAPP with PCA and PTCA
Fabrication and electrochemical studies of DSSCs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Cochin University of Science and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Cochin University of Science and Technology
Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank Cochin University of Science and Technology (CUSAT) for the support through SMNRI project and STIC & Department of Physics for the analysis. Inter University Center for Nanomaterials and Devices (IUCND) for the characterization of DSSCs. We also acknowledge DST-SERB for the financial support ( EMR/2016/005601 ). The partial financial support obtained from TDP/BDTD/34/2021 , UGC-SAP , DST-FIST , PLEASE and RUSA projects are also acknowledged. The authors would also like to thank Ms. Gopika Sabu, Research Scholar, DAC, CUSAT and the computer center at the Center of Integrated Studies, CUSAT for providing the computational facility.

We thank Cochin University of Science and Technology (CUSAT) for the support through SMNRI project and STIC & Department of Physics for the analysis. Inter University Center for Nanomaterials and Devices (IUCND) for the characterization of DSSCs. We also acknowledge DST-SERB for the financial support (EMR/2016/005601). The partial financial support obtained from TDP/BDTD/34/2021, UGC-SAP, DST-FIST, PLEASE and RUSA projects are also acknowledged. The authors would also like to thank Ms. Gopika Sabu, Research Scholar, DAC, CUSAT and the computer center at the Center of Integrated Studies, CUSAT for providing the computational facility.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption (A) and emission (B) spectrum of TAPP, GQD, PCA and PTCA
UV–Visible absorption spectra (A) and Steady state photoluminescence spectra (B) of TAPP in acetonitrile with increasing concentration of PCA (concentration ranges from 0 mM to 0.3 mM).
Stern-Volmer plot for PL quenching of TAPP in presence of increasing concentration of PCA from 0 mM to 0.3 mM.

(A) Time-resolved emission decay traces of TAPP in presence of PCA with an emission wavelength of 670 nm and 340 nm light emitting diode (LED) source (B) Overlapping spectra of emission and absorbance of PCA and TAPP to check the feasibility of FRET.
UV–Visible absorption spectra (A) and Steady state photoluminescence spectra (B) of TAPP in acetonitrile with increasing concentration of PTCA (concentration ranges from 0 mM to 0.3 mM).

(A) Time-resolved emission decay traces of TAPP in presence of increasing concentration of PTCA with an emission wavelength of 670 nm and 340 nm light emitting diode (LED) source. (B) Stern-Volmer plot for PL quenching of TAPP in presence of increasing concentration of PTCA from 0 mM to 0.3 mM.
Energy level diagram of the dyes calculated via experimental calculations.
Electronic distributions in the FMOs of the dyes TAPP:GQD, TAPP:PTCA, TAPP:PCA acquired from CAM-B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) level of theory.
AIM topology analysis of electron density indicating various critical points.
Current density-voltage (J-V) characteristics of fabricated DSSCs without and with blocking layer, A and B respectively, of a TiO2 nanocolloid over the FTO plates.

(A) J-V characteristics and (B) Nyquist plot of DSSCs fabricated with different concentrations of PCA on TAPP:PCA dye and with blocking layer. (C) Bode-Phase and (D) the equivalent circuit for the fitting of EIS data.
Lifetime analysis of TAPP:PCA blend by the varying concentration of PCA (3rd exponential fitting). a

head.ce:title: Fathoming into the dynamic interactions on Tetraaminophenylporphyrin hybrid systems via photophysical studies assisted by computational techniques and their role in dye sensitized solar cells

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Cochin University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Amongst the umpteen solar cells, dye sensitized solar cells by their low cost and eco-friendly nature, have a seat of their own. Photosensitizers is the pioneer component from which the entire process begins. Herein, Tetraaminophenylporphyrin hybrids in a series bearing with graphene quantum dot, 1-pyrenecarboxylic acid and perylenetetracarboxylic acid labeled as TAPP:GQD, TAPP:PCA and TAPP:PTCA were used as photosensitizers in DSSCs. Incipiently, the photophysical studies of these systems have been carried out and the feasibility of electron transfer from Tetraaminophenylporphyrin to the second component in the systems is acknowledged thereby. It is reckoned that photoinduced electron transfer with dynamic quenching mechanism is the impetus for the photophysical properties associated with TAPP:PTCA system, while it is Förster Resonance Energy Transfer in TAPP:PCA. Computational calculations with the aid of Density Functional Theory, Atoms-in-Molecules topology analysis and decomposition analysis elaborates the concept of non-covalent interactions within the hybrids. Following to that, DSSCs are fabricated and the appraised efficiencies of the devices show a pattern of TAPP:PCA > TAPP:PTCA > TAPP:GQDs. This work mainly focusses on the molecular level understanding of the electron transfer dynamics within the hybrid systems and thereby developing strategies in future for composing organic/inorganic hybrids with enhanced charge transfer ability, leading to higher efficiency.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Demand for energy is one of the main challenges faced by the humanity nowadays that can be effectively met by the use of solar energy. Sea change of light energy to electrical energy is achieved by a number of devices, out of which dye sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) are a gem [1]. Owing to the low cost and eco-friendly nature, DSSCs are best alternatives to conventional solar cells [2]. The four components of DSSCs, viz. photoanode, photosensitizers, electrolyte and cathode, act like wheels which roll the cell through the cycle of light absorption to the generation of electrical energy. Among these, photosensitizers are widely scrutinized owing to the fact that the execution of the entire cyclic process is started from this single step of light harvesting and thereby play a vital part in the power conversion efficiencies of DSSCs. Even so, lack of stability and dye aggregation are the vital issues faced by the currently available sensitizers [3]. In light of this, we have focused on developing photostable and efficient materials as dyes for DSSCs. Besides that, the tough toil in the synthesis of dyes having absorption in near IR region affects the light harvesting. In this context, a new strategy called co-sensitization is developed to avoid the problems of dye aggregation and wide range of absorption, which is a combination of two or more dyes with complementary absorption. This makes the absorption spectrum more panchromatic and thereby extends the spectral response range [4–6].

Reports on porphyrin based solar cells substantiates its applicability as a photosensitizer. Ji et al. engineered a co-sensitizing dye with porphyrin and thieno[3,2-b]indole consummated the ever highest efficiency of 14.2% [7]. Similarly, functionalized porphyrins with fluorine, phenothiazine, benzothiadiazole, pyridyl groups and so on, are experimented as photosensitizers [8–11]. Yet, synthesis of such intricate molecules make them unfriendly to researchers. Hence, the demand for facile porphyrin derivatives and their modification via co-sensitization rises today. Our previous reports based on the interaction between porphyrin derivatives and GQDs proclaim their application as photosensitizers in DSSCs [12,13]. Likewise, pyrene and perylene derivatives have been affirmed as good light harvesting molecules by previous literature reports [14–17].

This work is intrigued with the photosensitizers from blends of TAPP with GQDs/PCA/PTCA. Photophysical studies of these porphyrin based hybrids have been carried out by dint of UV–Visible spectroscopy, photoluminescence spectroscopy and time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) analysis, which narrates the possible interactions in the ground and excited states of the three hybrids called as, TAPP:GQDs, TAPP:PCA and TAPP:PTCA. The electron transfer dynamics between TAPP and blending components are assessed by TCSPC technique and Stern-Volmer plots, which are authenticated from the energy level diagrams figured out from cyclic voltammetry. DFT calculations have been carried out to understand more about the intermolecular interactions within the hybrid systems, and found that these are hand in hand with experimental observations. After that these hybrids are applied as photosensitizers in DSSCs and measured their cell parameters. In comparison, TAPP:PCA the one with higher efficiency was further subjected to optimization study with respect to the component concentrations.

1-Pyrenecarboxylic acid (PCA) and 3,4,9,10-Perylenetetracarboxylicdianhydride (PTCDA) are purchased from TCI chemicals. All other chemicals and solvents were used as received from Sigma Aldrich, Spectrochem and Alfa Aesar, without further purification. Freshly distilled pyrrole was used for the synthesis of TAPP. The Spectro UV–Vis Double beam UVD-3500 spectrometer was used to record the UV–Visible absorption spectra in the range 200–900 nm, using acetonitrile as the solvent. Fluorescence Emission spectra were recorded on Horiba Fluorolog-3 fluorescence spectrometer with the same solvent used in UV–Visible spectral studies. Life time analysis was recorded on Horiba DeltaHub using 340 nm nano LED as the source and data analyzed by exponential curve fitting. Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) measurements were obtained on CHI660E model Instrument (Electrochemical workstation).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the interactions of TAPP with PCA and PTCA
ce:section.ce:para: Perylenetetracarboxylicacid (PTCA) was prepared from PTCDA as per the report of Ferrere et al. [18] Tetraaminophenylporphyrins (TAPPs) and GQDs were prepared as reported in our earlier reports [13]. The interaction between TAPP and PCA/PTCA was studied by various spectroscopic techniques. An increasing concentration of PCA/PTCA were added to the fixed concentration of TAPP. Ground state and excited state interactions were probed by UV–Visible spectroscopy and Fluorescence spectroscopy, respectively. Also, the time resolved emission decay measurements were used to monitor the mechanistic aspects of porphyrin hybrid interactions that occur in the short periods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and electrochemical studies of DSSCs
ce:section.ce:para: A blocking layer with TiO2 nanocolloid was drop casted over the FTO plate at first instance. Then the TiO2 paste was prepared by mixing TiO2 nano powder (anatase) with isopropyl alcohol (IPA) and TiO2 photoanode was prepared by the method of doctor blading. This anode was dipped in a dye solution containing 0.3 mM concentration of each component in acetonitrile-tertbutyl alcohol solvent mixture, for about 22 h. Later the anode was taken outside and washed with acetonitrile to remove excess dye. Platisol coated as a layer over FTO plate was taken as the counter electrode. In the end, these two electrodes were sandwiched after injecting a drop of iodine/iodide electrolyte and are fixed to each other by binder clips. The current density-voltage (J-V) of the fabricated DSSCs were measured under AM 1.5G (100 mWcm−2) simulated using a solar simulator and perused using Keithely 2400 digital source meter. The active area was fixed at 0.25 x 0.25 cm2.

Computational results are acquired by the method of CAM-B3LYP and 6-31G(d,p) basis set for calculating the electronic structure. Gaussian 09 program package was used to carry out all the theoretical calculations. The geometries were fully optimized without any symmetry constraints. For all the calculations of GQD, we considered 13 rings with a number of –COOH and –OH groups, corresponding to the molecular weight of GQD obtained. The Eg was calculated at the same level of theory. Non-covalent systems were designed from the optimized geometries of each system. Binding energies and energy partitioning using simple decomposition analysis were calculated using Gaussian calculations. AIM analysis on non-covalent interactions and the associated plots were constructed using Multwfn program [19].

The photophysical studies of both TAPP and GQD are discussed in our earlier report [13]. Ergo, the PCA, PTCA and their combination with TAPP have been tackled in the current studies. The pyrene (PCA) and perylene (PTCA) derivatives showed typical absorption peaks at 243, 280, 349 & 383 and 370, 474 & 555 nm respectively, attributed from their π to π* and n to π* transitions (Fig. 1 A). Similarly, the photoluminescence spectra of PCA and PTCA showed peaks at 388 & 407 nm and 519, 558 & 605 nm respectively (Fig. 1B), which are in correlation with earlier reports [20,21]. To know more about the light harvesting ability of these molecules their molar extinction coefficients (MECs) were measured. MEC values of these dyes directly represents the rate of light absorbed by the material. Herein, Table S1 shows the MEC values of both PCA and PTCA, corresponding to their respective absorption wavelengths. Among them PCA shows highest value of 4.14x104 at 243 nm, while PTCA have 2.37x102 at 474 nm, which evinces the expedient absorption ability of PCA. Fluorescence decay analysis done via TCSPC proclaims the lifetime values of 7.08 ns and 4.29 ns for PCA and PTCA respectively.

Hither, the interaction of PCA and PTCA with TAPP was investigated by various spectroscopic techniques like UV–Visible spectroscopy, photoluminescence spectroscopy and time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) experiments. The study is conducted by the incremental addition of PCA/PTCA to fixed concentration of TAPP solution in acetonitrile solvent.

A) TAPP:PCA hybrid system. Looking into the UV–Visible absorption spectra in the case of TAPP:PCA hybrids, heeded that there is no characteristic ground state interaction between PCA and TAPP (Fig. 2 A). PL spectrum was recorded at an excitation of 425 nm and heeded the changes at a wavelength of 670 nm, as shown in Fig. 2B. The intensity of TAPP experiences a steady decrease on increasing the concentration of PCA, as similar to the case of TAPP:GQD as reported before [13]. Nevertheless, the percentage of quenching in TAPP:PCA is about 8% with a concentration range of PCA increases from 0 mM to 0.3 mM. This steady state quenching was appraised with the aid of Stern-Volmer plots. It suits best with a polynomial fitting (Fig. 3 ) and affirms an effective excited state interaction between TAPP and PCA.

Further, the supporting evidences for the quenching mechanism was gathered from steady state lifetime decay analysis. DAS6 software was used to fit the curves with a tri exponential decay equation for TAPP:PCA. A uniform enhancement for the lifetime plots of TAPP was observed for the increasing addition of PCA (Fig. 4 A). The calculation parameters of exponential fitting, average lifetime and non-radiative rate constant (knr) are given in the supplementary information (equation S1). Also, the kinetics of electron transfer within the nanohybrids are tabulated in Table 1 . In the TAPP:PCA system an increasing trend of lifetime values on the incremental addition of PCA to TAPP, which in turn prompted to check the feasibility of Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) between PCA and TAPP, because during energy transfer an enhanced lifetime value leading to augmented PL intensity, is observed at certain cases for a fluorochrome acceptor [22].

In order to know the energy transfer pathways, the overlapping spectra between the absorption and emission of both PCA and TAPP as in Fig. 4B was drawn. As evident from the figure, there is an effective overlap betwixt the emission of PCA and absorption of TAPP, which shows the feasibility of FRET. Consequently PCA acts as a FRET donor and TAPP as a FRET acceptor. This overlap of spectra indicates the ability of the energy lost from excited donor while coming back to ground state, which has been used to excite the acceptor. The extent of overlap is denoted as overlapping integral (J(λ)). Herein the J(λ) is derived as 1.028 × 1010 M−1cm−1nm4, obtained with the aid of a|e − UV–Vis-IR Spectral Software and the calculated energy transfer efficiency is 13.65% at 408 nm for 0.3 mM addition of TAPP to PCA from equation (1).
(1)
E = 1 −
F
D A
F D
where FD and FDA are the relative fluorescence intensity of donor PCA without and with the presence of acceptor TAPP, respectively [22].

The changes in the lifetime value of FRET donor was also scrutinized here. Fig. S4 displays the TCSPC spectra of PCA with increasing concentration of TAPP, which is concordant with the earlier reports [22]. Along with this study, the PL quenching of PCA in presence of TAPP is recorded and given as Fig. S3. The figure shows that there is a quenching behaviour for the emission of PCA on the incremental addition of TAPP, which further affirms the feasibility of energy transfer from PCA to TAPP. Along with FRET, the possibility of photoinduced hole transfer from TAPP to PCA cannot be discarded which could be the reason for the slight emission quenching behaviour as shown in Fig. 2B [23,24]. Thusly, the charge transfer dynamics in TAPP:PCA hybrid system is a combination of both FRET from PCA to TAPP and hole transfer from TAPP to PCA. These findings were supported by the energy level diagram plotted from Cyclic Voltammetric studies and optical bandgaps (Fig. 7) in the succeeding portion.

B) TAPP:PTCA hybrid system. Probing the ground state interactions on TAPP:PTCA hybrid system, a very petite change is identified as shown in Fig. 5 A. Close perusal of the figure discerns the presence of an isobestic point at 445 nm, which is the sequel of decrease and increase in the Soret (425 nm) and Q bands (658 nm, 563 nm & 520 nm) respectively. Herein the presence of isobestic point is an evidence for the existence of equilibrium between ground state TAPP and PTCA in solution with their corresponding complex, [TAPP:PTCA]. Next, the excited state electronic interactions were recorded by the same procedure as that of TAPP:PCA system and noted the changes in the PL peaks of TAPP which is given as Fig. 5B. PL intensity of TAPP faces a steady decrease with increase in the concentration of PTCA as in the case of TAPP:PCA. In spite of that the two systems show differences in their quenching efficiency i.e., the percentage of quenching in TAPP:PTCA is 22% (concentration of PTCA ranges from 0 mM to 0.3 mM).

Thenceforth, from the steady state lifetime decay analysis of TAPP:PTCA, a bi exponential decay equation was used to fit these curves in DAS6 software. Changes in the lifetime values of TAPP in presence of PTCA are paradoxical to the case of PCA. That is, a uniform quenching for the lifetime plots of TAPP was observed for the increasing addition of PTCA (Fig. 6 A). Similarly, electron transfer kinetics within this nanohybrid is tabulated in Table 2 . From the table, the reduction in average lifetime value of TAPP is evident which stipulates that the quenching is dynamic and shows the feasibility of photoinduced electron transfer from TAPP to PTCA.

Herein, Fig. 6B shows the Stern-Volmer plots corresponding to the fluorescence and lifetime quenching, fitted using equation (2).
(2)
F 0
F
=
(
1 +
K D
[
P T C A
]
)
(
1 +
K S
[
P T C A
]
)
where KD is the dynamic & KS is the static quenching constants, while F0 & F are the PL intensities of donor without and with the quencher respectively. These plots suit best with a polynomial fitting and affirms an effective donor-acceptor interactions. In short, from both ground and excited state interactions on TAPP:PTCA, arrived to a conclusion that the system exhibits photoinduced electron transfer (PET) with combined static and dynamic quenching mechanism.

Later, the relative non-radiative electron transfer from excited TAPP to PTCA is computed by equation S1. The change in lifetime value is also reflected in the knr values, which has a gradual increase from 5.13 x 109 to 37.46 x 109 sec−1, indicating an effective electron transfer. These findings were also supported by the energy level diagram plotted from Cyclic Voltammetric studies and optical bandgaps (Fig. 7).

Concurrently, in our earlier report on TAPP:GQD hybrid system, we noticed that there was no characteristic ground state interactions within the nanohybrid. Whereas an artful electron transfer from excited state TAPP to GQDs is observed in PL spectra. Thus the electron transfer dynamics within TAPP:GQD system, indicates a PET with dynamic quenching mechanism [13].

The energy levels of all the individual components are calculated from their corresponding cyclic voltammograms and the optical bandgaps are acquired from the intersection wavelength of absorption and emission spectra. The calculation parameters of HOMO/LUMO energy levels and optical band gaps are given in the supplementary information (equation S2). That are summarized in Table S2 and the plotted energy level diagram is given as Fig. 7.

The supporting facts of experimental observations are endowed through theoretical calculations. Gaussian 09 program package is used for the purpose by the hybrid method of CAM-B3LYP and 6-31G(d,p) basis set of density functional theory (DFT). These energy levels (ELs) are tabulated in Table S3. The ELs of both GQD and PTCA are lower as compared to the ELs of TAPP and that favors the feasibility of PET as reckoned earlier. In contrast to this, the LUMO of PCA has a comparable energy with the LUMO of TAPP that hinders the chances of PET and enables the FRET. These all are in agreement with the observations of interaction studies.

In order to perceive the possible interactions within these hybrid ones, a theoretical inquisition is conducted using the same method as above used for optimization. A pictorial representation of the relevant FMOs of three hybrid systems are shown in Fig. 8 . From the figure, it is evident that the charge distributions on HOMO are localized on TAPP, while LUMO is on GQD and PTCA respectively for TAPP:GQD and TAPP:PTCA hybrid systems. This indicates that the TAPP acts as a donor and GQD/PTCA as acceptors of the PET process in the respective hybrid systems. Meanwhile, in the case of TAPP:PCA both HOMO and LUMO are localized on aminoporphyrin. All these inferences are in good agreement with the experimental evidences. Also, the TAPP:GQD and TAPP:PTCA hybrids exhibit stacking (π-π) interactions along with electrostatic interactions that stand up for the cofacial orientation of their hybrid components as per Fig. 8. In contrast, the TAPP:PCA system possess higher electrostatic interaction between the oxygen of –CO/hydrogen of –OH in the carboxylic group of PCA, with the interior hydrogens/nitrogens of the porphyrin moiety. Also it was found that all the three systems have very good stabilization/interaction energy between the components in the hybrid system and their interaction energy values are tabulated in Table S4 among which TAPP:PTCA shows a higher stabilization energy of −33.9286 kcal/mol. While, the other two systems have lower and comparable interaction energies with each other.

Atoms-in-Molecules (AIM) topology analysis of electron density (Fig. 9 ) indicates many critical points within TAPP:GQD and TAPP:PTCA hybrid systems. However, the analysis shows that the number of such critical points is only a few within TAPP:PCA in comparison, emphasizing its lower interaction energy. Moreover a scatter plot of reduced density gradient given as Fig. S6 indicate similar nature of favorable van der Waal's interaction of TAPP with GQD & PTCA, while it is negligible with PCA. In case of H-bonding interactions, it is found to be higher in TAPP:GQD in comparison with the remaining two. Nevertheless, with more steric interactions TAPP:GQD exhibits a much lower binding energy among the three. These observations on different kinds of interactions are in agreement with the interaction energy trend according to Table S4. In order to quantify the steric interactions we did a simple decomposition analysis by combined fragment wavefunctions, from which it is computed that TAPP:GQD system has more destabilizing steric energy of 12.26 kJ/mol (i.e, the sum of electrostatic, Pauli's repulsion & exchange-correlation energy change) than the other two.

In order to understand the photoconversion efficiency of three hybrid systems, these were taken as photosensitizers in DSSC and the J-V characteristics of the cells were analyzed. At first the fabrication was done without blocking layer of TiO2 nanocolloid over the FTO plate. It was observed that among the three dye hybrids, TAPP:PCA system achieved a higher efficiency as demonstrated in Table 3 . Later, it was found that the cells with blocking layer (i.e., a layer of TiO2 nanocolloid over the bare FTO plate) shows a better performance than cells without blocking layer. That is, the cell developed from standard Ru(II) dye, N719 attained an efficiency of 4.13% initially, without blocking layer and it reaches to 6.23% in presence of blocking layer. On that account our further studies were carried out with nano TiO2 coated FTO plate. Hence, the FTO plate was decorated with a layer of TiO2 nanocolloid prepared by the hydrothermal method from the precursor, titanium isopropoxide (TTIP), which acts as a blocking layer in the fabrication of DSSCs. Fig. 10 depicts the J-V characteristics of the cells without (A) and with (B) blocking layer over TiO2 photoanode, based on the different dyes and their corresponding photovoltaic parameters are shown in Table 3. In the case of cells with blocking layer, amidst of three, TAPP:PCA with Jsc = 0.09 mA, Voc = 0.59 V and η = 0.31% shows better performance. While, TAPP:PTCA and TAPP:GQD systems exhibited a lower performance with an efficiency of 0.16% and 0.08% respectively.

The high current density (Jsc) and efficiency (η) values result from the excellent light harvesting ability of photosensitizers as evident from previous reports [25,26]. Herein, the higher efficiency of the TAPP:PCA hybrid system is in line with the higher molar extinction coefficient for PCA (2 × 104 M−1cm−1 at 350 nm in acetonitrile) than that of PTCA and GQD. Likewise, it is figured out that nanomaterials like GQD possess trap states, which as per the reports are some of the hypothetical energy levels that exist in between valence bands and conduction bands [23]. Their presence will lead to the trapping of conducting electrons transferred from excited TAPP to GQDs and that adversely affects the cell efficiency. As per the report elsewhere, the cooperation of the adjacent –COOH groups in the 3rd & 4th position as well as 9 & 10th positions of perylene are necessary to allow strong adsorption on TiO2 surface. But it is also reported that for the sensitization of nanocrystalline TiO2 with PTCA a titled/disordered geometry for the binding is adopted [27,28]. Herein, the strong interaction that occurs between TAPP and PTCA, which is demonstrated through the experimental as well as the theoretical evidences, hinders the feasibility of the geometry change for PTCA which in turn lowers the adsorption of it on TiO2 surface.

Quantitative evidence is acquired from the binding constants, calculated from BH plots of GQDs, PCAs and PTCAs with TiO2, given as Fig. S8. They are 7.84x102 M-1 and 9.94x102 M-1 for GQD-TiO2 and PCA-TiO2 respectively, while the case of PTCA does not give any significant binding value. The higher binding constant of PCA is in agreement with the higher efficiency of TAPP:PCA system. The photographs of dye adsorbed TiO2 coated photoanodes (Fig. S9), shows that each hybrid system imparts distinct colors to the layers after adsorption. This stipulates the variance in the adsorption of dyes that are in correlation with the binding constants. Also the color distribution in these topographic images is uniform, thereby assures the uniform distribution of our dyes over the whole TiO2 layer. Over and above on comparing the absorption spectra (Fig. 1A) of these three hybrid systems with their DSSC characteristics the higher efficient system, TAPP:PCA covers the entire range of absorption spectrum from 200 to 700 nm. Furthermore, in light of the known facts, the polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon, pyrene with extensive delocalization of electrons owns a hydrophobic nature that curbs the recombination of excited electrons with electrolyte and thereby augments the photocurrent [29]. In a nutshell, among the three dye hybrids advantages of PCA over the GQD and PTCA, make the TAPP:PCA dye as a better one for DSSCs.

As a part of the optimization of TAPP:PCA dye, variations in the molar ratio of the component dyes (PCA:TAPP = 0.5:1, 1:1, 1.5:1), were done to find out the better ratio of dyes to get good efficiency. In this study, the concentration of TAPP is kept constant and changes are made in the concentration of PCA. Their J-V characteristics are shown in Fig. 11 A and the corresponding cell parameters are tabulated in Table 4 . It was noted that on increasing the concentration of PCA, transpire a drastic change in Jsc values whereas a very petite change is identified in Voc values. These changes in the current density there by enhances the efficiency.

Electrochemical functionalities of a DSSC, chiefly the interfacial charge transfer processes can be well explained via Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS). This was conducted under dark conditions with a forward bias equivalent to Voc. The Nyquist plots in Fig. 11B and Bode plots in Fig. 11C for varying ratios of PCA:TAPP dyes are figured out from EIS, fitted by using the equivalent circuit in Fig. 11D. In Nyquist plots, the diameter of the semicircle increases in the order of PCA2 < PCA3 < PCA1, which directly stipulates the increase in recombination rate [29]. This shows that the intermolecular charge transfer and charge injection rate escalates in the reverse order, i.e., PCA2 > PCA3 > PCA1.

Likewise the electron lifetimes are reflected from Bode-phase plots using the following equation,
(3)
τ e
=
1
ω min
=
1
2 π
f min
They are 19.29, 23.37 & 50.36 ms for PCA2, PCA3 & PCA1 respectively. The lowest lifetime of electron in PCA2 is noteworthy, as it shows lowest recombination rate and moderate PCE on DSSC.

In summary, this work enlightens towards the molecular level understanding of the electron transfer dynamics of tetraaminophenylporphyrin based hybrid systems and their photosensitizing performance on DSSCs. The akin porphyrin dyads such as TAPP:GQDs, TAPP:PCA and TAPP:PTCA have been prepared at first. On moving through the various photophysical studies, it has been shown that there exists an effective interaction within these hybrids, in different ways. In the case of GQD and PCA, there exists no ground state interaction, but PTCA shows a significant interaction with TAPP in its ground state. All the three have a uniform quenching in the fluorescence emission intensity of TAPP by the incremental addition of the second component, which is a symbol of effective interactions in the excited state. That is the differences in their charge transfer dynamics were noted that TAPP:GQD follows dynamic quenching, TAPP:PTCA shows a combined static and dynamic quenching and TAPP:PCA accompanies a FRET mechanism. Theoretical investigation with the aid of CAM-B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) method also coincides with the experimental observations. Application of these hybrids as photosensitizers shows an order of photoconversion efficiency as TAPP:PCA > TAPP:PTCA > TAPP:GQDs in DSSCs. The lower efficiency of TAPP:GQD system is attributed to the presence of surface/trap states present in GQD, whereas in TAPP:PTCA, the strong interaction between TAPP and PTCA hinders the binding of PTCA with TiO2, leading to lower PCE. These findings in our study open up new pathways for the designing of organic/inorganic hybrids with enhanced charge transfer ability, which leads to higher efficiency of DSSCs.

Athira Pallikkara: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft, Data curation, Visualization. Kala Ramakrishnan: Validation, Resources, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation. Lakshmi Srinivasan: Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Visualization. Elambalassery G. Jayasree: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Data curation.

The partial financial support obtained from TDP/BDTD/34/2021, UGC-SAP, DST-FIST, PLEASE and RUSA projects are also acknowledged.
